During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:八剣神社 (諏訪市)]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)

[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:八剣神社 (諏訪市)]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)]]

The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa City)}}[[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] revision.text:
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Buildings in Suwa, Nagano]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}} [[Category:Default {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:History of {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yatsurugi Shrine |image = [[File:八剱神社.jpg|270px|Yatsurugi Shrine, Worship Hall]] |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Kowada 13-18 |coordinates = {{Coord|36|02|26|N|138|07|14|E|region:JP-20_scale:30000|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (Yachihoko no Kami)
{{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}} (Nihon Take Mikoto)
{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} (Homuda Wake no Mikoto) |type = {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine Sub-Shrine |festival = [[July 5]]
[[December 5]] }} '''Yatsurugi Shrine''' (やつるぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Formerly classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}, it enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} as Yachihoko no Kami, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. It serves as a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine. == History == Originally located on Takashima in {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}}, the shrine was historically built using funds raised through shipbuilding taxes on Lake Suwa. During the construction of {{ill|Takashima Castle|ja|高島城}} by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}, the shrine was relocated to its present location. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, lords of Takashima Domain, venerated the shrine as a guardian of their castle. Since the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, it has been worshiped as the tutelary deity of Kowada Village. == Suwa Lake Crossing Ritual == The shrine is notable for its unique ritual, the '''Lake-Crossing Ritual''' (みわたりしんじ). Commonly referred to as the "Observation of the Lake-Crossing Ritual," it is performed by the shrine's {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職}} (Shinto priests). (See also {{ill|Lake Suwa#Omiwatari|ja|諏訪湖#御神渡り|御神渡り}}). When the "Omiwatari" phenomenon is observed on Lake Suwa, the shrine convenes an extraordinary council to determine the date for the observation ceremony. Prior to the ceremony, the chief priest and other shrine officials place sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}}) on their household gates and undertake a period of ritual purification ({{ill|Shōjin kessai|ja|精進潔斎}}). On the day of the ceremony, they perform a purification rite (修祓) at the shrine before heading to Lake Suwa. At the lake, the priests inspect the ice formations, including the primary (一之御渡), secondary (二之御渡), and Saku no Miwatari (佐久之御渡) crossings, as well as both lower and upper shorelines. Upon returning to the shrine, a "Report Ritual" (奉告祭) is conducted to inform the enshrined deities of the lake's conditions. Based on these observations, predictions about the year’s harvest, societal fortune, and climate are made. The results are compiled into a "Report Document" (注進状) and submitted during the "Lake-Crossing Report Ceremony" (御渡注進式) at the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. The shrine then forwards the information to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} and the [[:en:Japan_Meteorological_Agency|Japan Meteorological Agency]].During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, these reports were submitted to the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. The most recent instance of this ritual occurred in [[2013]], with the observation on January 22, the ceremony on January 25, and the report submission on February 17. In years when the "Omiwatari" is not observed, the ritual still takes place, with a report stating "No crossing was observed." In such cases, Lake Suwa is referred to as "the opening sea" (明けの海). == Notes == == References == * *Temples and Shrines in Shinshu* (1981), {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}} * {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一}}, ed. *The Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 9 – Mino, Hida, and Shinano* (2000), Hakusuisha. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of shrines in Suwa|ja|諏訪市#神社}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} * {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} * {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}} == External Links == * [https://shrineheritager.com/yatsurugi-shrine/ Shrine-Heritager: Yatsurugi Shrine (Suwa)] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Faith Project] - History of {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} {{citation needed|Yatsurugi Shrine}} [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]]
